title: Green wood bowl rough-out
subtitle: Carving a bowl blank from a riven green-wood slab with carving axe and inshave
excerpt: A bowl roughed from a riven green slab takes half the effort of carving from a fully seasoned blank because green wood cuts freely. The blank is shaped close to the target dimensions and left to dry; once stable, final carving and finishing remove only a small amount of material from a piece that will not move further. This project covers riving the slab, shaping the outside with a carving axe, and hollowing with an inshave.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for axe-carved vessel traditions. Scandinavian carved-vessel tradition (kuksa and naver forms) for the riven-slab bowl rough-out method.

glossaryTerms.definition: Split along the grain with a froe. A riven bowl slab follows the grain exactly and dries with less checking than a sawn board.
A heavily curved drawknife used with both hands to hollow the interior of bowls and chair seats. Pulled toward the body in long strokes across the grain of the hollow.
A small curved-sole plane used to refine the inside of a bowl or chair seat after the inshave has done the bulk removal. Leaves a smoother surface than the inshave alone.
Freshly cut timber at high moisture content. Green wood carves readily; the bowl rough-out is completed green, then dried before final finishing.

projectSchedule.title: Rive and rough-out the bowl
projectSchedule.body: Split the log to a slab, axe the outside form, inshave the hollow to near-finished depth.

projectSchedule.title: Season the rough-out
projectSchedule.body: Coat the end grain with raw linseed oil and leave in a ventilated shed. The bowl will distort slightly as it dries; this is expected.

projectSchedule.title: Final carving and finish
projectSchedule.body: Carve the inside and outside to final surface, sand if desired, apply finish oil.

content.text: The green rough-out method divides bowl carving into two short sessions separated by a drying period. The first session does the hard work while the wood is
and cuts freely; the second session refines a stable, dry blank that will not distort during use. A
slab follows the grain exactly, which keeps drying movement predictable and reduces the chance of a crack splitting the bowl during the seasoning period.

A straight-grained green log, 20 to 25 cm in diameter and 30 to 40 cm long. Sycamore, alder, lime, and birch all carve cleanly and dry to a pale stable finish; cherry gives a warm colour and carves well in green state but is harder once dry. Avoid any log with visible spiral grain or knots in the bowl area.
A froe and beetle to split the slab. A carving axe to shape the outside profile. An
or wide gouge to clean up after the inshave. An India oilstone and leather strop; green fibres blunt edges quickly. Raw linseed oil for end-grain sealing before the drying period.
Carving axe cuts away from the body with the workpiece on a low block. The inshave pulls toward the body; hold the rim of the slab with the free hand, not the interior being cut.

Halve the log with the froe and beetle, then rive one half to produce a slab about 10 to 12 cm thick and wide enough for the target bowl outline with 3 cm margin on each side. The flat riven face becomes the base of the bowl; the curved outside of the half-log becomes the dome of the exterior.

Place the slab on a low block with the flat riven face down. Mark the bowl plan outline on the upper surface. With the carving axe, work from the high centre toward the rim to remove the rounded bark-side surface to a gentle dome. Make cuts across the grain toward the rim rather than along the grain in the direction of the rim; long-grain cuts risk splitting through the wall. Leave 15 mm minimum wall thickness at this stage.

Clamp the slab to a bench or hold it between the feet. Pull the inshave across the grain of the hollow in long strokes, removing shavings from the centre outward. Cut at 45 degrees to the grain if the inshave tears rather than cutting clean. Hollow to 5 to 6 cm depth for a shallow serving bowl. Finish the inside surface with the travisher in overlapping passes to remove the inshave ridges. Strop the inshave edge every five minutes; green wood blunts the inshave faster than any other carving stage.

Coat all end-grain surfaces of the rough-out with raw linseed oil using a rag. End grain dries five to ten times faster than face grain; sealing it slows moisture loss through the fastest-drying surfaces and reduces the risk of a through-crack. Stack the bowl hollow-side down on two sticks in a ventilated, unheated shed. Leave for two months at 12 to 15 mm wall thickness, longer if the walls are thicker.

The dried rough-out will have moved: the rim will be oval rather than round, the base may have cupped, and the walls will have settled to their final form. Final carving removes the axe and inshave tool marks, corrects any uneven wall thickness, and brings the surface to the finish depth. A dried blank carves more slowly than the green rough-out but will not move again once the finish is applied.
A deeper bowl for bread or fruit needs a log diameter of 30 cm or more. Drying time extends to three to four months at 20 mm wall thickness.

For a food-use bowl, the final finish after drying and sanding should be raw linseed oil (three coats, one per day) or food-grade walnut oil. Do not use boiled linseed oil for bowls used with food; the driers in boiled linseed oil are not food-safe.
Re-oil the inside of the bowl when it looks dry. Do not soak in water or put through a dishwasher; hand-wash and dry immediately.

items.symptom: The bowl develops a crack through the wall during drying
items.cause: End grain was not sealed before drying, or the blank was dried in a heated room rather than an unheated shed.
items.fix: Coat all end-grain surfaces with raw linseed oil and move to a cool ventilated space. A crack that has started cannot be closed; carve a clean V-channel along it and accept it as a feature, or glue with a water-resistant wood glue if structural integrity is needed.

items.symptom: The inshave tears the grain rather than cutting
items.cause: The edge is dull, or the pull direction runs against the grain.
items.fix: Strop the inshave and change direction; try pulling at 45 degrees to the grain. If tearing continues on a fresh edge, the grain in this area reverses and must be addressed from the other side.
